name: Cuencas Mineras and Puerto de Majalinos Loop
name_original: Subida al Puerto de Majalinos, Eljuve - circular por las cuencas mineras
description: This difficult 75.7-mile road cycling route ascends to Puerto de Majalinos, offering panoramic views and a challenging ride through the Iber
region_intro: Road cycling around Estercuel, located in the Andorra-Sierra de Arcos region of Teruel province, Spain, offers diverse landscapes for cyclists. The area features the mountainous terrain of the Serranía de Montalbán, providing significant elevation changes. River valleys, such as those formed by the Guadalopillo and Escuriza rivers, offer contrasting flatter sections. The region also encompasses parts of the Maestrazgo Cultural Park, known for its varied geological formations and natural beauty. Best road cycling routes around Estercuel The most popular road cycling route is Ejulve - Aliaga Maestrazgo Loop, a 77.7 miles (125.0 km) difficult trail that takes 6 hours 33 minutes to complete. This route traverses the varied terrain of the Maestrazgo Cultural Park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Cuencas Mineras and Puerto de Majalinos Loop, a difficult 75.7 miles (121.9 km) path. This route explores areas near former mining landscapes and includes significant mountain passes. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de Villarluengo – Mountain Pass loop from Ejulve, a 72.8 miles (117.2 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 6 hours 21 minutes. Road cycling around Estercuel is defined by mountain ranges, river valleys, and diverse geological formations. The network primarily offers challenging options for experienced road cyclists. The routes in Estercuel are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 180 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Estercuel's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Estercuel?
A: Estercuel offers a diverse landscape for road cyclists. You'll find challenging climbs and descents in the mountainous terrain of the Serranía de Montalbán. In contrast, the river valleys of the Guadalopillo and Escuriza rivers provide flatter, scenic sections. The region also encompasses parts of the Maestrazgo Cultural Park, known for its varied geological formations and natural beauty, including areas like the 'Barrancondo' with its pine forests and diverse birdlife. You might even encounter unique landscapes near former mining areas.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Estercuel?
A: The road cycling routes around Estercuel are primarily challenging, with 10 out of 12 routes rated as difficult and 2 as moderate. There are currently no easy routes listed, indicating the area is best suited for experienced road cyclists looking for significant elevation changes and demanding rides.

Q: What are some notable natural features I can explore by road bike in the Estercuel area?
A: The region boasts several natural features ideal for road cycling. You can ride through the Serranía de Montalbán mountain range, offering rewarding views and challenging ascents. The valleys of the Guadalopillo and Escuriza rivers provide scenic routes, often following winding paths. The Maestrazgo Cultural Park is rich in geological formations, and areas like the 'Barrancondo' offer tranquil natural environments with Aleppo and resin pines, holm oaks, and diverse birdlife.

Q: What kind of cultural or historical attractions can I visit along the road cycling routes?
A: You can incorporate several cultural and historical stops into your road cycling trips. The 16th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5361244" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Santa María del Olivar&lt;/a&gt;, located just 4 km from Estercuel, offers impressive views from its hilltop position. The town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5361246" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estercuel&lt;/a&gt; itself, with its medieval origins and the Santo Toribio parish church, is a pleasant place to visit. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5290366" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ejulve&lt;/a&gt; area is a common starting point for many routes and offers local charm.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints or natural landmarks accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, the region offers several scenic spots. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5361244" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Santa María del Olivar&lt;/a&gt; provides impressive vistas of the Escuriza River valley and surrounding mountains. Mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1637345" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Majalinos&lt;/a&gt; are significant landmarks and offer panoramic views. The Cueva Foradada Reservoir also presents a tranquil destination for a scenic break.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Estercuel?
A: The road cycling routes in Estercuel are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 5.0 stars from over 3 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the challenging nature of the rides, and the beautiful landscapes encountered, particularly within the Maestrazgo Cultural Park and near the former mining areas.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Estercuel?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Estercuel are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e961402763/ejulve-aliaga-maestrazgo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ejulve - Aliaga Maestrazgo Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970627231/cuencas-mineras-and-puerto-de-majalinos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuencas Mineras and Puerto de Majalinos Loop&lt;/a&gt; are popular circular options, allowing you to start and finish in the same location after exploring the diverse regional landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Estercuel?
A: While specific seasonal data isn't provided, the Teruel province, where Estercuel is located, actively promotes cycling tourism. Generally, spring and autumn offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling in this region of Spain, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder conditions of winter. Checking local weather forecasts before your trip is always recommended.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through unique geological areas?
A: Absolutely. The Maestrazgo Cultural Park, which Estercuel is part of, is renowned for its breathtaking landscapes shaped by millions of years of geological history. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e961402763/ejulve-aliaga-maestrazgo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ejulve - Aliaga Maestrazgo Loop&lt;/a&gt; traverse this varied terrain. Additionally, the 'desmontes mineros' to the west of Estercuel, former open-pit lignite mines, offer a unique, almost 'science fiction' landscape of geological interest, providing a distinctive backdrop for a road cycling route.

Q: How long do road cycling routes in Estercuel typically take to complete?
A: The road cycling routes around Estercuel are generally long and challenging. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31472608" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Villarluengo – Mountain Pass loop from Ejulve&lt;/a&gt; typically takes around 6 hours and 21 minutes, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e961402763/ejulve-aliaga-maestrazgo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ejulve - Aliaga Maestrazgo Loop&lt;/a&gt; can take about 6 hours and 33 minutes. These durations reflect the significant distances and elevation gains involved.

Q: Are there any routes that include significant mountain passes?
A: Yes, the region is known for its mountainous terrain and includes significant mountain passes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970627231/cuencas-mineras-and-puerto-de-majalinos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuencas Mineras and Puerto de Majalinos Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically explores areas near former mining landscapes and includes notable mountain passes. Another example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10418414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Cristóbal Pass – Puerto de Majalinos loop from Gargallo&lt;/a&gt;, which features two prominent passes.
tips: Throughout the year the village receives visitors to see the Marco Canyon and the cave paintings in the Martín River Natural Park (UNESCO). Alcaine has had mines for a long time, but in the Franco era, and due to a lack of energy, the Gil family's mine was buried and a swamp was created to use the water to make electricity (hydroelectric).

faqs.question: How difficult is the Ascent to Puerto de Majalinos cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered difficult and requires a very good level of fitness. The climb to Puerto de Majalinos features irregular gradients, with sections reaching up to 10%, especially towards the final kilometers from Ejulve. It's recognized as a 1st category climb in the cycling community.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on paved roads, which are generally narrow and tranquil. However, some segments, particularly on the Aliaga side, may have rough asphalt. The climb itself is irregular, with varying inclines and some false flats interspersed with steeper ramps.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summer can be very hot, and due to past fires, there is limited shade, making it crucial to carry plenty of water. Winters can be cold and potentially snowy at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route is on public roads, it's generally not suitable for cycling with dogs due to the challenging nature and length of the ride. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer designed for pets and are accustomed to long distances and varying road conditions.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this area?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are required to cycle this route. However, the area is part of the Río Martín Cultural Park. While there are no fees for general access, visitors should respect local regulations regarding environmental protection and cultural heritage sites within the park.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trailhead for this route?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts from the town of Ejulve (Eljuve). You can find parking within the town itself. As it's a road cycling route, there isn't a traditional 'trailhead' like for hiking, but Ejulve serves as a convenient starting point with amenities.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: Cyclists will experience an untamed and solitary environment with pine forests, ravines, and distinctive rock formations. Key highlights include the town of Alcaine, the Hermitage of Santa Ana, and the Old Pass of Las Traviesas. The views from the summit of Puerto de Majalinos offer impressive panoramic outlooks.

faqs.question: Does this route pass through significant mining heritage sites?
faqs.answer: While the region around Ejulve has a significant mining history, this specific road cycling route primarily focuses on the natural landscape and the challenging climb to Puerto de Majalinos. You'll be in an area with a mining past, but direct engagement with prominent mining infrastructure on the paved circuit itself is not a primary characteristic.

faqs.question: What gear should I bring for this racebike route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, bring a well-maintained racebike, appropriate cycling apparel, a helmet, and plenty of water, especially during warmer months due to limited shade. Nutrition, a repair kit, and navigation tools are also highly recommended.

faqs.question: Are there any alternative starting points or ways to tackle the climb?
faqs.answer: Yes, while the route described starts from Ejulve, the climb to Puerto de Majalinos can also be approached from the Aliaga side. This ascent is longer (12.2 km) with a slightly gentler average slope (3.8%) but still features rough asphalt in sections and very little traffic. Choosing your starting point can alter the challenge and scenery.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with or are in close proximity to other trails. You may encounter parts of the Ruta de la Muela, Vuelta a la Muela, and the [CIMA TE08] Majalinos * Ejulve. The area also features parts of the Sendero del Río Martín.
